question: Operations management is primarily concerned with:
options: Managing a company's financial assets and investment portfolio

Designing, managing, and improving the processes that transform inputs (materials, labor, capital, information) into outputs (products and services) — the core of how a company actually creates and delivers value
Developing new products and conducting market research
Managing relationships with shareholders and public investors
explanation: Operations is about transformation: inputs → process → outputs. A restaurant's operations transforms raw ingredients (inputs) through cooking processes into meals (outputs). Amazon's operations transforms orders (inputs) through fulfillment centers and logistics processes into delivered packages (outputs). McKinsey transforms client problems (inputs) through consulting processes into strategic recommendations (outputs). Every organization has operations — the processes of value creation. Operations management: designing those processes to be efficient, quality-focused, and flexible enough to meet customer demand.

question: The 'triple constraint' in operations (also called the iron triangle) refers to:
options: The three types of inventory: raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods

The tension among Cost (efficiency), Quality (meeting standards), and Speed (time to deliver) — improving one typically requires trade-offs in the others; the classic example: a restaurant can be fast-food cheap (low cost, quick, acceptable quality) or fine dining (high quality, high cost, slow) or fast-casual (moderate on all three) — but not simultaneously the cheapest, fastest, AND highest quality option
The three stages of manufacturing: design, build, and test
The three primary operations decisions: make, buy, or partner
explanation: Operations trade-off: Toyota demonstrated that lean manufacturing can significantly improve all three simultaneously, but there are still limits. McDonald's: optimizes for cost and speed, accepts standardized (not artisanal) quality. Rolex: optimizes for quality, accepts high cost and slow production. Amazon Prime same-day: optimizes for speed (same-day) by accepting higher cost (premium warehouses in urban areas). The operational strategy should align with what target customers most value — which of the three dimensions they're willing to pay a premium for.

question: A company that builds a product and then sells it from finished-goods inventory uses which production strategy?
options: Make-to-order

Make-to-stock (also called push manufacturing): production happens before customer orders arrive; goods are produced based on demand forecasts and held in inventory; when customers order, they receive from existing stock; advantages: fast delivery (items already made); risks: overproduction (waste if forecasts are wrong) or stockouts (insufficient inventory if demand exceeds forecast); used by: consumer goods manufacturers (P&G making Tide detergent), fast-food restaurants (making burgers before orders), commodity producers
Assemble-to-order
Engineer-to-order
explanation: Make-to-stock: the standard for commodity/high-volume products with predictable demand. P&G doesn't wait for you to order Tide before making it — they produce continuously based on demand forecasts and replenish retail shelves. Risks: overproduction (perishable food waste, fashion obsolescence) or stockouts (lost sales, customer frustration). The trade-off: inventory carrying cost and obsolescence risk vs. fast delivery. Amazon's fulfillment centers hold make-to-stock inventory of millions of products — enabling fast delivery but requiring accurate demand forecasting.

question: Dell Computers pioneered 'configure-to-order' manufacturing in the 1990s: customers specified their computer configuration online, then Dell assembled it from component inventory. What operational advantage did this create vs. make-to-stock competitors?
options: Configure-to-order was slower and more expensive, but consumers preferred the customization

Dell's configure-to-order advantages: (1) Eliminated finished-goods inventory of complete computers (which became obsolete as component prices dropped 2% monthly); (2) Deferred cash payment to components (cheaper than finished computers in inventory); (3) Enabled mass customization — each customer's computer had exactly the components they ordered; (4) Faster cash cycle — customers paid before Dell paid suppliers; competitors carrying complete computer inventory held depreciating assets; Dell's operational model turned inventory into a competitive advantage by eliminating it, while reducing the cash tied up in finished goods
Configure-to-order had no operational advantages — Dell succeeded only due to lower prices
All computer manufacturers switched to configure-to-order within one year of Dell's success
explanation: Dell's Direct Model (Michael Dell's autobiography): the computer market in the 1990s had components declining in value 2% per month (Moore's Law-driven price drops). Make-to-stock computer manufacturers (Compaq, HP) carried 60–90 days of finished-goods inventory — which lost 4–6% of its value while sitting in warehouses. Dell's configure-to-order: components held (not finished computers), assembly starts only after order received (3–5 day lead time). Negative cash conversion cycle: customers paid on order; suppliers paid on net-60 terms. By 2000, Dell had $2B in cash from its operational model while competitors had depreciating inventory.

question: A hospital's operations differ from a manufacturing plant's operations in which fundamental ways?
options: Hospitals have no operations — they are purely service organizations

Service operations (hospital) vs. manufacturing operations: (1) Intangibility — hospital delivers healthcare (expertise, procedures, care) not physical goods; cannot inspect or store the output before delivery; (2) Customer co-production — patient participation in diagnosis and recovery is essential; (3) Perishability — an unused operating room slot is permanently lost revenue; cannot inventory 'patient care hours'; (4) Variability — each patient's needs differ significantly; standardization is harder than manufacturing; (5) Simultaneous production and consumption — the patient is present during the service; this changes operations management of capacity, quality control, and the role of the 'customer' in the process
Hospital operations are identical to manufacturing operations
Service operations are less important to manage than manufacturing operations
explanation: IHIP characteristics (Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability) drive fundamental differences in service vs. manufacturing operations. Hospital: each patient interaction is unique (heterogeneity), quality can't be inspected before delivery (inseparability), unused capacity is permanently lost (perishability), and patients actively participate in their own care (co-production). These characteristics explain why hospital operations management focuses on: scheduling (maximizing OR utilization), standardizing where possible (surgical checklists, medication protocols), and quality through systems rather than end-of-line inspection.

question: Amazon's fulfillment center design and process optimization is a core competitive advantage. How does operations excellence at Amazon translate directly to customer value and financial performance?
options: Amazon's fulfillment operations are a cost center that doesn't differentiate the company from competitors

Operations → customer value chain: (1) Order accuracy (operations quality) → customer trust and fewer returns (customer value + lower return cost); (2) Delivery speed (operations efficiency) → Prime's same-day/next-day promise (customer value); (3) Scale and density (operations design) → lower cost per package → enables Amazon's low prices (customer value); (4) Inventory availability (operations forecasting) → fewer stockouts → higher customer satisfaction; Amazon's fulfillment operations IS the product for many customers — Prime members pay $139/year primarily for the delivery operation; operations isn't a back-office function at Amazon, it's the front-line customer value driver
Amazon's fulfillment operations are entirely outsourced to third parties
Fast delivery speed is primarily driven by Amazon's technology, not its physical operations
explanation: Amazon's operations-as-competitive-advantage: in most companies, operations is a cost center that management tries to minimize. At Amazon, operations is the customer value proposition — fast, accurate, low-cost delivery IS the product that Prime members pay for. Amazon's robotics investments (Kiva acquisition 2012: $775M), fulfillment network (200+ warehouses), and delivery infrastructure are all operational investments that create customer value directly. The lesson for business students: operations isn't the boring back room — in many industries, operational excellence is the primary source of competitive advantage and customer value.

question: A call center processes 10,000 customer calls per day with average handle time of 6 minutes. It currently employs 200 agents on 8-hour shifts. The operations manager wants to reduce cost per call by 15%. What operations levers are available?
options: The only way to reduce cost per call is to reduce agent wages

Operations levers for cost reduction: (1) Reduce average handle time (AHT) — better agent training, improved knowledge base, call routing to specialists reduces AHT from 6 to 5 minutes = 17% more calls with same agents; (2) Increase first-call resolution — resolving issues once eliminates repeat calls; (3) Self-service deflection — IVR, web chat, FAQ reduces calls reaching agents; (4) Workforce management — better shift scheduling matches agent supply to call volume patterns (avoid overstaffing at low-volume hours); (5) Technology (AI-assisted response suggestions) reduces time spent searching for information; reducing volume + improving handle time can achieve 15% cost reduction without wage cuts
Call center cost is fixed and cannot be reduced through operational changes
Reducing agent count is the only available operations lever for cost reduction
explanation: Call center operations management: the key metrics are AHT (average handle time), occupancy rate (% of time agents are handling calls vs. idle), and first-call resolution rate. Cost per call = (total agent cost + overhead) / total calls handled. To reduce cost per call: increase calls per agent (reduce AHT, improve scheduling) OR reduce calls reaching agents (self-service, prevention). Amazon dramatically reduced call center costs by: (1) Making ordering so intuitive fewer customers needed help; (2) Building 'Where's My Order?' tracking to reduce 'where's my package?' calls (their largest call volume). Operations improvement often requires analyzing WHY customers contact you before optimizing HOW you handle those contacts.

question: Toyota's Production System (TPS) — the foundation of lean manufacturing — identifies seven types of waste (Muda). Which of the following is a correct application of waste identification?
options: Waste in lean manufacturing refers only to physical material scraps and defects

The seven wastes (TIMWOOD): Transportation (moving products unnecessarily), Inventory (excess stock), Motion (unnecessary worker movement), Waiting (idle time for people or machines), Overproduction (making more than needed), Overprocessing (doing more than the customer requires), Defects (rework, scrap, returns); Example: a hospital nurse walking 500 meters to retrieve supplies = Motion waste; a retailer holding 90 days of seasonal inventory = Inventory waste; a restaurant preparing 500 meals for 400 customers = Overproduction waste; each waste type is an operations improvement target
Lean manufacturing only applies to automotive manufacturing and cannot be applied to services
Waiting time for workers is not a form of waste since workers must be available for demand
explanation: Toyota's 7 wastes (TPS) applied broadly: Virginia Mason Hospital system (Seattle) applied TPS to hospital operations using 'RPIW' (Rapid Process Improvement Workshops) — identifying the 7 wastes in nurse workflows, reducing nurse walking distance by 50%, and freeing nursing time for patient care vs. supply retrieval. This reduced costs, improved quality, and increased patient satisfaction. Lean thinking: any activity that consumes resources but doesn't add customer-perceived value is waste. In software development: unnecessary features (overprocessing), bugs (defects), and unused features (overproduction) are wastes. TPS principles are universal beyond manufacturing.

question: A company manufactures 1,000 units per week with a defect rate of 2%. It discovers that fixing defects at the end of the production line costs $50/unit, but catching defects at the source (early in the process) would cost $5/unit. What is the operations management principle at work?
options: End-of-line inspection is always more cost-effective since not all defects need fixing

Prevention and early detection cost principle: (1) Current cost: 1,000 × 2% × $50 = $1,000/week in end-of-line rework; (2) Early detection cost: 1,000 × 2% × $5 = $100/week; (3) Prevention (root cause elimination) would cost even less per unit caught; the principle: quality costs escalate as defects progress through the process; a flaw caught in design costs 1x; in prototype costs 10x; at manufacture costs 100x; at customer site costs 1,000x; this is Juran's cost of quality principle and the rationale for building quality IN at the source rather than inspecting it OUT at the end
Defect rates below 5% do not require operations management attention
All defects should be accepted if the cost of prevention exceeds the cost of fixing them
explanation: Cost of quality (Philip Juran): the further downstream a defect is caught, the more expensive to fix. Rule of Ten: catching a defect at each subsequent stage costs 10x more. Boeing discovered this: design changes cost $1M; changes in manufacturing tooling cost $10M; changes after aircraft delivery cost $100M+. The same principle in software: a bug in requirements costs $100 to fix; in design $1,000; in development $10,000; in testing $100,000; in production $1,000,000+ (user impact + reputational cost). The operations implication: invest in upstream quality controls (statistical process control, design reviews) even though downstream catches are more visible.

question: A restaurant chain wants to expand from 10 to 100 locations. What operations challenges does this scaling create that don't exist at 10 locations?
options: Scaling from 10 to 100 restaurants creates no new operational challenges if recipes are the same

Process standardization + consistency across multiple locations: at 10 locations, the owner/founders can personally train each manager and maintain quality through personal oversight; at 100 locations, consistency requires: documented SOPs for every process (recipe cards, service standards, cleaning protocols); training systems that scale (training trainers, not just training employees); supply chain capable of reliably supplying 100 locations with consistent ingredients; management structure with regional oversight; quality measurement systems (mystery shoppers, customer satisfaction metrics) to detect deviation; the transition from 10 to 100 locations is fundamentally an operations standardization and systems-building challenge, not a marketing challenge
The only operational challenge of scaling is hiring more staff
Each additional restaurant location requires proportionally more overhead, making 100 locations less profitable than 10
explanation: McDonald's scaling case study: Ray Kroc's genius was operational standardization, not food innovation. The Speedee Service System: every McDonald's makes fries the same way, at the same temperature, for the same duration. This required: process documentation, equipment standardization (every fryer identical), supplier standardization (Simplot supplies identical potatoes), and Hamburger University (training system). Without operations standardization, each location drifts in quality — the customer's experience at location 87 differs from location 4. Quality inconsistency destroys brand trust at scale. The operations challenge of scaling: making the 100th location as consistent as the 1st.

question: UPS and FedEx have invested billions in route optimization algorithms, predictive maintenance, and package tracking systems. How does technology investment in operations translate to competitive advantage?
options: Technology investments are a cost with no differentiation value since competitors can copy them

Operations technology advantage is durable when it creates learning loops: UPS's ORION (On-Road Integrated Optimization and Navigation) algorithm analyzes 55,000 route segments per driver per day, reducing driving distance and fuel cost by $300M–$400M/year; the algorithm improves as it processes more delivery data — creating a learning advantage that compounds as the network grows; predictive maintenance reduces downtime; real-time tracking (customer-facing) reduces 'where's my package?' calls; the key: technology investments build data advantages that are hard to replicate because competitors starting later have less historical data and less algorithm training time
Package delivery is a commodity and technology investment provides no sustainable advantage
FedEx and UPS compete entirely on price, making technology investment irrelevant
explanation: Operations technology as competitive moat: ORION (deployed 2012) reduced UPS's right turns (which require waiting for traffic to clear, wasting time/fuel) dramatically. The algorithm improvement flywheel: more deliveries → more data → better algorithm → lower cost → lower prices or higher margin → more volume → more data. This is a data-network effect in operations. The competitive advantage: a competitor starting the same algorithm today would have 10+ fewer years of training data. Technology investments in operations often create durable advantages when they generate proprietary data that improves with scale — this is different from buying the same software package as competitors.

question: Starbucks experienced significant quality and consistency degradation as it expanded from 1,000 to 13,000 stores between 1998 and 2008. Howard Schultz returned as CEO and had baristas stop using automatic espresso machines (faster) and return to manual espresso pulling. What does this reveal about the operations-quality trade-off?
options: Manual processes are always higher quality than automated processes in food service

Speed/efficiency and quality sometimes require genuinely different process designs: automatic espresso machines increased throughput (operationally efficient) but removed the artisan ritual that was core to the Starbucks experience — the visible craftsmanship of a barista pulling espresso was itself part of the product (customer experienced the care); the trade-off: automatic machines were operationally superior on throughput metrics but destroyed the service experience value; this forced Starbucks into a choice that most operations trade-off frameworks can't capture: 'do we optimize for the easily measured metric (throughput) or the harder to measure but more strategically important metric (experience quality)?'
Starbucks' quality problems were caused by barista hiring issues, not equipment changes
Automated processes always produce better quality results than manual processes in food service
explanation: Starbucks' 2008 crisis (Schultz's memo 'The Commoditization of the Starbucks Experience'): automatic espresso machines solved the throughput problem but eliminated the theater — the visible process of baristas carefully pulling shots was the experience customers paid for. Schultz's diagnosis: operational efficiency had degraded the product. The choice: accept lower throughput (longer lines, higher labor cost) to restore quality, or maintain speed at the cost of brand experience. Schultz chose quality over speed — closing all 7,100 US stores for 3.5 hours on Feb 26, 2008 to retrain baristas. The lesson: metrics matter — if you measure throughput but not experience quality, you optimize toward the measured metric at the cost of the unmeasured one.

question: Apple's manufacturing strategy involves: designing its own chips (M-series processors), controlling industrial design, but outsourcing physical manufacturing entirely to Foxconn and TSMC. What operations strategy logic drives this 'design in, manufacture out' approach?
options: Apple outsources manufacturing because Apple cannot afford to build factories

Vertical integration at the value-creating nodes: Apple controls the processes that create differentiation (chip design, industrial design, software integration) and outsources the processes that are commoditizable (physical assembly at scale); Apple's A/M-chip design teams create the competitive advantage (no competitor has equivalent chip performance-per-watt); but physically assembling iPhones doesn't require Apple's unique capabilities — Foxconn's scale, labor, and manufacturing expertise does this more efficiently than Apple could; the decision rule: vertically integrate where you have irreplaceable advantage; outsource where a partner has superior capability; this concentrates Apple's capital and talent where ROI is highest
Apple should vertically integrate its entire manufacturing to protect quality
All technology companies should design and manufacture their own products
explanation: Apple's supply chain strategy: Apple spends $10B+/year on chip R&D (vertical integration at the differentiating step) but doesn't own a single assembly factory. The logic: Apple's chip design advantage is worth $10B R&D investment because it produces chips no competitor can match. Building Foxconn-equivalent assembly capacity would cost $50B+ and produce something Foxconn already does better (labor management at scale in Shenzhen). The operations principle: integrate at nodes where your capabilities are scarce and differentiating; outsource at nodes where commodity partners have structural cost advantages. TSMC manufactures Apple's chips on the same equipment competitors use — but Apple's chip architecture is proprietary.

question: A company's operations review shows: delivery reliability = 72% on-time (customer expectation: 95%), product defect rate = 3.2% (industry benchmark: 0.8%), and unit production cost 18% above industry average. The CEO asks the operations team to prioritize improvements. Using an operations strategy framework, what is the correct prioritization?
options: Always prioritize cost reduction since it directly impacts profitability

Order-winner vs. qualifier analysis: (1) Delivery reliability at 72% vs. 95% expectation = a 'qualifying failure' — the company is below the threshold to win orders (customers may stop ordering due to unreliability); this is a must-fix. (2) Defect rate at 4x industry benchmark = quality failure; rework and returns are increasing cost AND damaging customer relationships; fix immediately. (3) Cost at 18% above industry = competitive disadvantage but not an immediate qualifier failure; cannot sustainably compete long-term but won't lose customers tomorrow. Priority order: (1) Delivery reliability (existential), (2) Quality (cost + customer), (3) Cost reduction once quality foundation is established
Cost is always the most important operations priority
All three issues require equal investment and cannot be prioritized
explanation: Operations competitive priorities (Terry Hill's order-winner/qualifier framework): qualifiers = minimum requirements to compete (below this threshold, customers don't consider you); order-winners = the criteria on which you beat competitors. Delivery reliability below 72% means customers are actively choosing competitors due to operational failure — this is existential. Defect rate 4x benchmark = customer dissatisfaction + hidden cost (rework, returns, warranty claims). Unit cost 18% above average = structural disadvantage that erodes margin but doesn't immediately lose customers. Fix in sequence: qualify first (reliability, quality), then optimize (cost). Trying to reduce cost while quality is broken typically fails because cost reduction targets the wrong problem.

question: Two pizza chains operate in the same market. Chain A focuses on delivery speed (30-minute guarantee, GPS routing, dedicated delivery fleet). Chain B focuses on ingredient quality (fresh dough, local produce, premium cheese). Can both chains be operationally successful simultaneously?
options: No — a competitive market forces all competitors to optimize for the same metrics

Yes — different operations strategies serve different customer segments: Chain A's operational design (routing optimization, delivery fleet management, rapid assembly) serves customers who value time most; Chain B's operational design (sourcing relationships, prep processes, fresher supply chain) serves customers who value quality most; they have different operational core competencies, different supplier relationships, different metrics (Chain A: minutes per delivery; Chain B: ingredient freshness), and they target different customer occasions (Chain A: 'I'm hungry now'; Chain B: 'I want a premium dinner'); successful markets have multiple viable operations strategies because customers have different values and are willing to pay for different benefits
Speed and quality cannot both be operations advantages in the same market
Operations strategy must be identical for all competitors in the same market
explanation: Operations strategy alignment with customer value proposition: Porter's competitive strategies (cost leadership vs. differentiation) require different operations designs. Chain A (speed focus): investments in fleet management software, delivery routing, rapid assembly process design, location density to reduce delivery radius. Chain B (quality focus): investments in sourcing relationships, freshness management, skilled dough-making staff, premium ingredient supply chain. The two chains cannot share an operations design — attempting to optimize for both simultaneously (fast AND fresh) risks becoming mediocre at both. Successful market segmentation means different players can thrive with different operational designs serving different customer needs.
